source_paper_title: Tight Running Times for Minimum ℓq-Norm Load Balancing: Beyond Exponential Dependencies on 1/ϵ
context:
Section 1 (Introduction), page 2; reiterated in Section H (Conclusion), page 46

It remains an problem to obtain tight running times when m = \(\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta \in (1/2, 1]\).
title: Tight PTAS complexity for minimizing \(\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\) when \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) with \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\)
background_latex: We consider scheduling \(n\) jobs on \(m\) identical parallel machines. Job \(j\) has processing time \(p_j>0\). A schedule assigns each job to a machine. For a schedule, let \(C_i=\sum_{j\to i} p_j\) be the total load on machine \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\). Fix a constant \(q>1\). The objective is to minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\). A polynomial-time approximation scheme (PTAS) is a family of algorithms that, for any \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), returns a \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximate schedule. We measure the running time as a function of \(n\), \(m\), and \(\varepsilon\), and we focus on the regime where the number of machines scales with \(\varepsilon\) as \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for some fixed \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the tight (up to \(\tilde O(\cdot)\) factors and lower-order terms) optimal running time dependence on \(\varepsilon\) of a PTAS for \(P\|\|\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\) in the regime \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for fixed \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\); specifically, either
- design a PTAS with provably optimal (as a function of \(\varepsilon\)) running time in this regime, or
- prove a matching lower bound (e.g., under ETH) ruling out any asymptotically faster PTAS in this regime.
latex:
We consider scheduling \(n\) jobs on \(m\) identical parallel machines. Job \(j\) has processing time \(p_j>0\). A schedule assigns each job to a machine. For a schedule, let \(C_i=\sum_{j\to i} p_j\) be the total load on machine \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\). Fix a constant \(q>1\). The objective is to minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\). A polynomial-time approximation scheme (PTAS) is a family of algorithms that, for any \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), returns a \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximate schedule. We measure the running time as a function of \(n\), \(m\), and \(\varepsilon\), and we focus on the regime where the number of machines scales with \(\varepsilon\) as \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for some fixed \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\).

Determine the tight (up to \(\tilde O(\cdot)\) factors and lower-order terms) optimal running time dependence on \(\varepsilon\) of a PTAS for \(P\|\|\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\) in the regime \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for fixed \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\); specifically, either
- design a PTAS with provably optimal (as a function of \(\varepsilon\)) running time in this regime, or
- prove a matching lower bound (e.g., under ETH) ruling out any asymptotically faster PTAS in this regime.
